section.title: Be Holy
section.sectionRationale: Having celebrated the living hope, Peter draws the first great therefore of the letter. The mercy just described now calls for a whole new way of life: minds set on grace, conduct marked by holiness and reverent fear, and love flowing from hearts reborn by God's enduring word. Indicative has become imperative; who they are becomes how they live.
section.thesis: Because they have been redeemed by the precious blood of Christ and born again through the living word of God, believers set their hope fully on coming grace, live holy and reverent lives as the children of a holy Father, and love one another deeply from pure hearts.
section.frameNote: Three movements: set your hope and be holy, verses 13 to 16; live in reverent fear as the redeemed, verses 17 to 21; and love deeply as those born of the enduring word, verses 22 to 25.

units.label: Set your hope, be holy
units.note: as He who called you is holy

verses.text: Therefore prepare your minds for action. Be sober-minded. Set your hope fully on the grace to be given you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.
As obedient children, do not conform to the passions of your former ignorance.
But just as He who called you is {{hagios|holy}}, so be holy in all you do,
for it is written: “Be holy, because I am holy.”

units.label: Live in reverent fear as the redeemed

verses.text: Since you call on a Father who judges each one’s work impartially, conduct yourselves in reverent fear during your stay as foreigners.
For you know that it was not with perishable things such as silver or gold that you were {{lytroo|redeemed}} from the empty way of life you inherited from your forefathers,
but with the precious blood of Christ, a {{amnos|lamb without blemish or spot}}.
He was known before the foundation of the world, but was revealed in the last times for your sake.
Through Him you believe in God, who raised Him from the dead and glorified Him; and so your faith and hope are in God.

units.label: Love deeply, born of the enduring word
units.note: the word of the Lord stands forever

verses.text: Since you have purified your souls by obedience to the truth so that you have a genuine love for your brothers, {{agape|love one another deeply}}, from a pure heart.
For you have been {{anagennao|born again}}, not of perishable seed, but of imperishable, through the living and enduring {{logos|word}} of God.
For, “All flesh is like grass, and all its glory like the flowers of the field; the grass withers and the flowers fall,
but the word of the Lord stands forever.” And this is the word that was proclaimed to you.

hagios.gloss: Holy, set apart, belonging to God and reflecting His pure character. God calls His people to be holy because He Himself is holy, quoting Leviticus. Holiness is not first a list of rules but a whole life set apart to God, matching the character of the Father who called them.

lytroo.gloss: To redeem, to ransom, to buy back by paying a price. The word comes from the marketplace and from the freeing of slaves. Believers were redeemed from an empty way of life, not by silver or gold, but by the costly blood of Christ, so they now belong wholly to God.

amnos.gloss: A lamb without blemish or spot, the flawless sacrificial animal required in Israel's worship. Christ is that lamb, sinless and without defect, whose precious blood redeems. The image joins the Passover lamb and the sacrificial system, all fulfilled in Him.

agape.gloss: Self-giving love that seeks the good of others. Peter commands believers, whose souls are purified for genuine love, to love one another earnestly and from a pure heart. Because they share one new birth, love within the family of God is the fitting overflow of the truth they have obeyed.

anagennao.gloss: To be born again, begotten anew by God. Here the new birth comes through the living and enduring word of God, an imperishable seed. The Christian's new life springs from God's own word and shares its permanence, unlike all that is merely human and passing.

logos.gloss: Word, the living and enduring word of God through which new birth comes and which stands forever. Peter contrasts it with grass and flowers that wither. This word, quoted from Isaiah, is the very gospel preached to the readers, permanent and life-giving where all flesh fades.

basic.context: Peter now turns from what God has done to how we should live. Because we have such a hope, we are to get our minds ready, keep clear-headed, and fix our hope on the grace coming when Jesus returns. We are to be holy, like the Father who called us. We should live in reverent awe, remembering the enormous price of our rescue: not silver or gold, but the precious blood of Christ, the spotless Lamb. And because we have been born again through God's living word, we are to love one another deeply from the heart.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the first big therefore of the letter. Everything commanded here rests on the mercy already described. Holiness is not how we earn God's favor; it is the natural response of children to a holy Father who has redeemed them at infinite cost. Peter grounds each command in a gospel truth: be holy because God is holy, live in awe because you were redeemed by blood, love because you share one new birth.
basic.originalLanguages: God who called us is hagios, holy, and so we are to be holy. We were not lytroo, redeemed, with silver or gold, but by the blood of Christ, the amnos amomos, the lamb without blemish. Having been anagennao, born again, through the living logos, word, of God, we are to show agape, love, for one another from a pure heart.
basic.typology: The precious blood of the lamb without blemish fulfills the Passover lamb and the whole sacrificial system, where a spotless animal died to redeem. The call to be holy quotes Leviticus, where Israel was set apart; now the church is that holy people. And the enduring word that outlasts withering grass, drawn from Isaiah, is fulfilled in the gospel that gives new birth.
basic.christ: Christ is the spotless Lamb whose precious blood redeems us, known before the foundation of the world and revealed at the end of the ages for our sake. Through Him we believe in God, who raised Him and gave Him glory. He is both the ransom price and the one through whom our faith and hope rest in God.
basic.god: God is holy, and He calls His children to reflect that holiness. He is a Father, tender and near, yet one who judges each person's work impartially, so He is to be approached with reverent fear. He planned redemption before the world began and carried it out in Christ. And He is the God whose living word never fails, giving new and imperishable life.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 19 gives the command Peter quotes, be holy for I am holy. Exodus 12 shows the Passover lamb whose blood redeems. Isaiah 40 is the source of the withering grass and enduring word. And Romans 12 echoes the call not to conform to this world but be transformed.
basic.oneStory: God has always been forming a holy people, redeemed by sacrifice and shaped by His word. Israel was called to be holy and marked by the blood of the lamb; now the church is redeemed by the true Lamb, Christ, and born again through the enduring word. The call to holiness and love is the same ancient purpose reaching its fulfilment in the gospel.

context.literary: Verse 13 opens the letter's exhortation with therefore, drawing out the doxology of verses 3 to 12. The section is a chain of grounded commands: prepare, hope, be holy, live in fear, love. Each imperative is anchored to an indicative, and the unit closes with a quotation from Isaiah that seals the theme of the enduring word over against fading human glory.
context.historical: The readers had come out of a former way of life inherited from their forefathers, described as empty, likely a mix of pagan religion and inherited custom. The reminder that they were ransomed not by silver or gold but by Christ's blood would strike hard in a world where redemption prices, especially of slaves, were familiar. Living as foreigners names their ongoing social displacement.
context.canonical: The passage weaves together Leviticus, Exodus, and Isaiah, showing the church as the continuation of God's holy, redeemed people. The Lamb without blemish gathers up the sacrificial system, and the enduring word links the gospel to the prophetic word that stands forever. Peter reads the whole Old Testament as fulfilled in Christ and applied to the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press the mercy of the gospel into transformed living: minds set on coming grace, lives marked by holiness and reverent fear because of the costly redemption in Christ, and deep mutual love flowing from the new birth through God's enduring word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This section is heavy with prescription, but every command is rooted in description. Be holy rests on God's holiness; live in fear rests on the price of redemption; love one another rests on the new birth. The imperatives are never bare rules; they are the fitting response to what God has already done.

debates.question: What is the reverent fear in which believers are to conduct themselves, verse 17?

views.label: Reverent awe before a holy Father
views.case: Since believers call on God as Father yet He judges impartially, the fear is worshipful reverence and seriousness about holiness, not cringing terror. It is the awe that befits children of a holy God.

views.label: A sobering awareness of coming judgment
views.case: Others stress that the Father judges each one's work, so the fear includes genuine accountability. Even the redeemed live coram Deo, before God's face, and take His assessment seriously.

debates.bearing: Both hold together love and reverence. Believers draw near as beloved children, yet never treat their holy Father casually; grace deepens, rather than dissolves, godly fear.

debates.question: Does born again through the word mean regeneration by the gospel or by Scripture generally?

views.label: The gospel word preached to them
views.case: Peter says the enduring word is the good news that was proclaimed to you, verse 25. New birth comes as the Spirit uses the preached gospel to bring dead sinners to life.

views.label: The living and enduring word of God broadly
views.case: Others note the word is called living and enduring, echoing Scripture's abiding power, so God's revealed word in its fullness is the imperishable seed of new life.

debates.bearing: The readings converge: God brings new birth through His word, supremely the gospel of Christ, and that word abides forever where all human glory fades.

study.originalLanguages: The call is to be hagios, holy, because the God who called us is holy, a direct echo of Leviticus. The ground of the call is redemption: we were not lytroo, ransomed, with perishable silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, the amnos amomos, the lamb without blemish or spot, the flawless sacrifice. Because we have been anagennao, born again, not of perishable seed but through the living and enduring logos, the word of God, we are to overflow in agape, earnest love for one another from a pure heart. That word, unlike withering grass and fading flowers, stands forever, and it is the very gospel preached to the readers.

typology.type: The lamb without blemish or spot, verse 19
typology.fulfillment: The Passover lamb and the spotless sacrifices of Israel, all fulfilled in Christ, whose precious blood truly redeems.

typology.type: Be holy, for I am holy, verse 16
typology.fulfillment: Israel's calling in Leviticus to be a holy people set apart, now realized in the church redeemed and reborn in Christ.

typology.type: The enduring word over withering grass, verses 24 to 25
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's word that stands forever, fulfilled in the gospel of Christ that gives imperishable new life.

study.christ: Christ is at the centre as the spotless Lamb whose precious blood is the ransom price of redemption. He was foreknown before the foundation of the world and revealed at the end of the ages for the readers' sake, showing that their salvation was always the plan. Through Him they believe in God, who raised Him from the dead and glorified Him, so that their faith and hope rest securely in God. He is both the costly sacrifice that redeems and the risen, glorified one through whom they come to the Father.
study.god: God is revealed as holy and as Father, a combination that shapes the whole passage. His holiness sets the standard for His children's lives; His fatherhood makes them near and dear. Yet He judges each one's work impartially, so He is approached with reverent fear as well as love. He planned redemption before creation and accomplished it in the blood of Christ. And His word is living and enduring, the imperishable seed of new life that stands forever when all human glory withers.

crossReferences.note: Be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy, the command Peter quotes to ground the call to holiness.
The Passover lamb must be without blemish, fulfilled in Christ the lamb without blemish or spot whose blood redeems.
All flesh is grass, but the word of our God stands forever, the passage Peter cites for the enduring word.
By God's mercies, offer yourselves and do not conform to this world, matching Peter's therefore and call not to conform to former passions.

study.oneStory: God has always been making a holy people for Himself, redeemed by the blood of a spotless lamb and shaped by His abiding word. What Israel foreshadowed at Passover and Sinai is fulfilled in Christ, the true Lamb, whose blood ransoms and whose gospel gives new birth. The scattered believers, called to holiness and love, are that holy people come of age, living out the ancient purpose of God as they await the grace to be revealed when Jesus appears.

quiz.q: On what basis does Peter command believers to be holy?

options.text: Because the God who called them is holy, be holy for I am holy.
options.note: Verses 15 to 16: God's own holiness is the ground and standard, quoting Leviticus.

options.text: Because holiness earns God's acceptance.
options.note: Holiness is the response to redemption already given, not the way to earn it.

options.text: Because their culture expected upright behavior.
options.note: The call is grounded in God's character, not social expectation.

options.text: Because they are children called to reflect their Father.
options.note: True and warm, though Peter states the ground explicitly as God's own holiness.

quiz.q: With what were the believers redeemed?

options.text: The precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or spot.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19: not silver or gold, but Christ's costly, spotless sacrifice.

options.text: The sincerity of their own repentance.
options.note: Redemption is by Christ's blood, received by faith, not purchased by repentance.

options.text: Silver and gold given generously.
options.note: Peter explicitly rules out perishable silver or gold as the ransom.

options.text: A costly sacrifice appointed by God before creation.
options.note: True, Christ was foreknown before the world, though the answer should name the precious blood itself.

quiz.q: What does reverent fear in verse 17 mean for the redeemed?

options.text: Worshipful awe before a holy Father who also judges impartially.
options.note: Verse 17 holds together nearness as children and seriousness about holiness.

options.text: A sober sense of accountability to the God who judges each one's work.
options.note: True and part of the meaning, though it should be joined to loving nearness as children.

options.text: Cringing terror that God might reject them at any moment.
options.note: They call on Him as Father; the fear is reverence, not dread of rejection.

options.text: A casual familiarity that treats God lightly.
options.note: The opposite of the reverent fear Peter commands.

quiz.q: Through what have believers been born again?

options.text: The living and enduring word of God, an imperishable seed.
options.note: Verse 23: new birth comes through God's abiding word, the gospel preached to them.

options.text: Their own decision to change their lives.
options.note: The new birth is God's work through His word, not self-reformation.

options.text: Perishable seed, like all natural birth.
options.note: Peter explicitly contrasts the imperishable seed with the perishable.

options.text: The truth they obeyed, which purified their souls.
options.note: Related and true, though the seed of new birth is named as the enduring word of God.

quiz.q: Why does Peter quote all flesh is like grass but the word of the Lord stands forever?

options.text: To show that the gospel word giving new birth is permanent while all human glory fades.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25: the enduring word outlasts withering grass, and it is the gospel preached to them.

options.text: To warn that life is short so they should enjoy it.
options.note: The point is the permanence of God's word, not seizing the day.

options.text: To say that nothing at all in this world lasts, not even God's promises.
options.note: The opposite: God's word stands forever though flesh fades.

options.text: To contrast fading human achievement with God's abiding word.
options.note: True, though the climax is that this enduring word is the very gospel they received.

questions: Peter tells us to set our hope fully on the grace coming when Jesus is revealed. What lesser hopes tend to capture your heart instead?
Holiness here means a whole life set apart to a holy Father. In what one area is God calling you to look less like your former way of life and more like Him?
You were redeemed not by silver or gold but by the precious blood of Christ. How does remembering that price shape the way you treat your daily choices?
Reverent fear and calling God Father belong together. Does your walk with God lean too far toward casual familiarity, or too far toward fearful distance?

Because you share one new birth, Peter calls you to love other believers deeply from the heart. Who is one fellow Christian you find hard to love, and what would earnest love look like toward them this week?

translationNotes.note: Prepare your minds for action renders a phrase that literally reads gird up the loins of your mind, the image of tucking in a robe to be ready to work or run.
Be holy, because I am holy quotes Leviticus 11:44 to 45 and 19:2, applying Israel's calling to the church.
Love one another deeply translates a word for earnest, stretched-out love; some manuscripts read from the heart, others from a pure heart, with the same call to sincere love.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 1:13-25. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
